speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry-- when did you marry, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. In May 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children yet while you were still in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you come to the United States? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had affidavits. My late wife had relatives in the States. And they actually sent the papers already to Hungary for us. Little did we know then that it is not only a matter of having an affidavit. You have to be under quota, and the Hungarian quota is very miserable. And the only way-- only reason we were able to come to the States was that President Truman brought a so-called DP bill in which 100,000 DPs, without quota, were able to come to the United States. And this is how we got to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where did you go to in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America we arrived to Tulsa, Oklahoma, because this is where my late wife's relatives lived. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to Tulsa, Oklahoma?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a mecca. Look, again, we came from concentration camps, DP camps, so everything was beautiful and wonderful, except we did not have any warmth, or did not get any feelings from the relatives, as we were so hungry for. And I'm not blaming them. It is just-- first of all, they are nobilities in Tulsa. One of the cousins, six times removed, was a colonel in the Air Force. The other one was a major in the Army. So for us, an American soldier was a demigod, so imagine such.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we arrived, they greeted us, told us, and then after two or three days, they made a party in our honor to introduce the [? greeners ?] to their-- and besides, we were, I think, the sixth or seventh of these DPs. We were uniques. So they were proud to show off the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the same time, the first speech that he gave me was, I know, children, you suffered a lot. But don't think that life was so easy for us either. We were standing in line for the gasoline. We had coupons for sugar. And there were certain things that we had to buy on the black market even. We looked at each other, with my wife, and said, [INAUDIBLE]. OK, go ahead, talk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So going back to the question, which was, how come the survivors did not want to talk? Because who would understand? If you got a reception, they said, yeah, I know, your problems and ours were about on the same level. And ours was not so unusual, either. I heard it since from many other survivors who heard similar remarks from their relatives regarding the conditions that they thought we were in, and the hard times that they went through here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And jumping a little, the first time we went to Israel was in 1968. And I remembered that I had those aunts and uncle in Palestine. So we stayed in King David Hotel and put an ad in the Hungarian paper that Andrew Gruenfeld from Gyongyos is here looking for relatives. Please call. So sure enough, within a day, I get a phone call from one of my aunts, who writes that I am in this kibbutz, Givat Haim, and it would be so nice if you could come and visit us. Next day, with great expectation, we went down to the kibbutz. My aunt looks at me. She did not remember me at all, because I was a little kid. I remember vaguely. But anyhow, nice reception from her. And she says, OK, stay here for lunch. Stay with us all day. I want you to meet my son. Her son is one of the leaders of the kibbutz. Comes for lunchtime, Amiram, a big, husky kibbutznik. His mother-- his mother brings him over. Here, these are your cousins from Los Angeles. Tell them shalom. He walks away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to my aunt, listen, I came 10,000 kilometers for a handshake? I want a little more than that from my cousin? Well, she says, he's a busy man. You know, these kibbutzniks are-- and she said, it's not unique by themselves. But stay here. I will take you over after dinner, after he comes back from work, and then we talk. So we go over to his house. House-- room, with a little kitchenette. It was then the kibbutz looked like that, even though this was a wealthy kibbutz. So I tell him, Amiram, I came from America to see you. You know that your grandfather was my grandfather. Your mother and my mother were sisters. You can't just shake me off like this. So he picks me up, practically. He takes me to the door, opens the order, and points out. You see? These are 600 people in this kibbutz. This is my mishpocha. Again, [INAUDIBLE] I kept on insisting that, look, I have to talk to you. He said, I know what you're going to tell me. And I know that I'm going to pieces if I listen to you. But go ahead, tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all night long we were talking. He was crying like a baby, the big, husky, tough, rough kibbutznik. And he said, I understand. And I told him, it is not a matter of being slaughtered. Under certain circumstances, people react the same way. The difference between a hero and a coward is one day of more hunger. And that's the truth. After a certain while, human beings cease to be human. They become animals, and instinct keeps you going.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I envied people who were able to sit down next to the road, pull their jacket over their heads, because they knew what is coming. We-- if we were thinking, we knew that this is a hopeless case. It's only another day of suffering. So why don't we put an end to it? Because the animal instinct kept us pushing. So anyhow, he said, just promise me one thing. He says, you will never tell my children this story, what you told me. I said, why? And this was in 1968, after the '67 war, when every Israeli felt that they are Superman. They can take care of-- and he said, but listen. We are surrounded with 100 million Arabs, and every one of our children has to feel that they have to-- they are able to take care of 100 Arabs. And if you will tell them that their great-grandparents and their great-uncles went to the slaughterhouse, they will lose their feeling of security, or even, he admitted, superiority.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there is another continuation to this story. In 1974-- from '68, at least every two years, we went to Israel. In '74, we are in Israel. My cousin Amiram-- since then, he came to America. We became like cousins should be, very close to each other. In '74, he calls me up. [? Bandi-- ?] that's my nickname-- I have to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes to the hotel. He says, I owe you an apology. Why do you owe me an apology? Because, he says, in '73, the Syrians captured two of his kibbutzniks. And they tortured them, and they made some radio announcements against Israel that, he said, I would have never believed that an Israeli would do.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So under the same circumstance, everybody becomes very similar. And that reminds me-- one more anecdote. In--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --1950, I was called to the draft.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And at that time, I lived in Tulsa, and in '51 came here. And went to the draft board. I was going to be inducted to the US Army during the Korean conflict.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have been 25 or 26 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I was married, but had no children, so I qualified. And [INAUDIBLE] a disqualification. But I was in Tulsa when I first read in the paper that people of this and this age have to report to the draft. I went to my cousin and asked him, do I have to really? He said, yeah, you guys are so helpless. He picks up the phone, calls the draft board. The guy tells him, yes, I have to. He says, that's all it takes. Now you know. I said, Elliott, you know what's the difference between you and me? He says, what? I said, that your father came over and not mine. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to this story-- where was I? You were here and you went into-- you were drafted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, drafted. And it starts out like little voting booths. In every booth, somebody else sits, and you get a folder starting out empty, and keeping where were you born, parents, when did they die. And you know, by then, my story is there, that they died in Auschwitz. And I tell them that I came from the concentration camp. I'm not very anxious to go [INAUDIBLE] sorry. Once you became an American, do we have to go there? Yeah, have to go, have to go. What can I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to the last station, and a guy says, oh, so you were in a concentration camp. And you have nightmares. And you scream at night. And you have horrible dreams, huh? Hmm, hmm, hmm. Sorry, 4-F.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] OK. At the same time, there is another guy with me who did not get through the same, evidently, and he was sent over to Korea. He's captured by the North Koreans. He becomes one of the announcers against the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: They exchange the prisoners. He's being court-martialed for treason, making statements against America. And in the court, the judge asks him-- the military court-- do you have anything to say, young man? Is there-- he says, yes, your honor. What is it? That I do not believe you have a right to sit in judgment over me unless you would have been in the same place, under the same circumstances. And the guy ultimately was discharged with honor, because they recognized, accepted it, that yes, you cannot sit here comfortably and pronounce judgment on somebody who was tortured, physically and mentally, and had to say it to save his life. So back to-- What did you do in Tulsa? What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a department store. I started out as a janitor. I became a shipping clerk, and then a salesman. But I was living under their thumb, constantly, I felt. Before I sneezed, I had to ask permission, so to speak, because how would a [? greener ?] know how to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to give me all these lectures. Very early one day, he calls me into his office. And you know, every so often he gave me lectures about how we Americans live. And he's a second-generation American himself. One day he's walking up and down, and he says-- every once in a while, he turns back. You follow me? You following me? And I thought that he really meant that I had to get up and walk behind him.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And got up and started to walk behind him. He turns around. And you should have seen that face of that utmost disgust and disappointment. Not only did I try to help, you're making fun of me? I said, how do I make fun of you? He says, you're walking behind me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I said, but I honestly thought that you meant it. But ever since then, he accepted it as a misunderstanding. But I was the Follow-Me Kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] That's what he called you, the Follow-Me Kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you decide to come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I developed there a hay fever that I could not help myself. I was sneezing, and it became almost an asthma. And I asked the doctor, what is it that I could do. He said, change climate. And it so happened that my late wife had a relative here, too. We came here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a family yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We did not have any children for 13 years after our marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we adopted two children, finally, because the women in the concentration camps were getting bromide that they should not have their periods. And some of them never got it back. And my wife was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you adopted two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to Los Angeles, what did you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [INAUDIBLE] I was a shipping clerk. Worked for a year and a half as a shipping clerk in a fire-fighting equipment company. And then I decided that won't take me anywhere. I saw advertising for upholsterers. Reported for upholsterer. I worked there for one day, and the foreman came in the evening, gave me my check. You are not an upholsterer.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Went next and saw another ad-- upholsterer. They asked, where did you work before?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I already had a background. I lasted a week there. And in a third place was a very decent foreman. He said, I know you've never been an upholsterer. You don't know the front end of a fabric-- [LAUGHS] --because I didn't. So we had put it upside-down, inside-out. But he started to teach me. I worked there for seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: As an upholsterer, yes. And after that, with another boy, I opened an upholstery shop, which-- but he was a very frum boy, a very religious boy, which I wasn't in those days. And I wanted to open up a store in front of the shop that we could sell our own products. But the store had to be open Saturday. We knew it. And he wouldn't go for it, so I sold my share to him. And at that time, I decided we are going to adopt children. But in those days, it was very difficult. You know, in California, there is, or was, at least, an unwritten law that the babies have to match the adoptive parents' faith, or at least they have to be mixed religion. And there were no Jewish kids for adoption in those days, because first of all, to have illegitimate children 40 years ago was a shame, unlike today. And somebody told us that, go out of LA County. In San Bernardino County, there are Jewish girls who, by mistake, become pregnant, go out until they have the children, and then they give it away for adoption. And it so happened we went to Fontana, San Bernardino County. And within a year and a half, we adopted two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was two months old, because he was a premature baby. And the other was one week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are your sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark and Larry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And now you have a very lovely--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I lost my wife in 1974 after eight years of illness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, when the war was over, most of us felt that nothing will ever shake us. Nothing will make us very happy, or nothing will make us very sad. And for a long time, it was like that. You sort of became apathetic, lost lust for life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But time is a great healer for everything. So as I said, my wife was the last person-- my mother [INAUDIBLE] that this marriage was really made in heaven, if there is such a thing. And we were very, very close together. In 1966, we discovered that she had a lump in one of her breasts. And after seven surgeries and eight years of suffering, she died. And that was after a time when we already had-- in 1960-- we adopted our first son in '61. The second-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --especially Larry, was very, very apprehensive. And they were-- you know, we were spoiling them, because we did not have them for so long. But when she got sick, they were pushed aside. We gave them to brother-in-laws or wherever, and I was spending my time with her in the hospital. And the younger one became very apprehensive. What kind of a God is this that is only [INAUDIBLE] my mother? You know, kind of coming back what I went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But in 1975, I met Yvette. I'm very lucky. Some people don't have even one successful marriage. I, thank God, have both of them, very, very lovely women. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a bit about God, because you've brought this up several times. You are a religious man now. Do you do this only because, as you said, this is how you wanted to raise your children? Or has your belief returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: A difficult question, if you want to answer honestly. First of all, I grew up in it, so I am very comfortable with it. Second, Yvette is a truly religious woman. Her children are even more religious. As we discussed this morning, her children are the generation who is more religious than the one before. And third, socially I feel very comfortable with it. I'm going to shul and keeping Shabbat, kosher. But is my belief is as it was as a child? No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know that there must be a God. But I don't know if I can accept-- not that I have a choice-- his ways as undisputable.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said before that if there was a God, he had to be either impotent or cruel. Do you see another aspect now?

speaker: interviewee
text: God's ways are-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --not questionable, according to the faith. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't say anything more, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: --like I said, I do not have the rock-solid belief like I used to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have peace of mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you don't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I am unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it because-- when don't you have peace of mind? Is it the stuff that all of us just live through in the daily Sturm und Drang of life? Or is there something else that disturbs your peace of mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, naturally it has a lot of influence on what I went through, of being deprived. My children did not have grandparents, which I see how important for a child's upbringing now that we have grandchildren. I find it unjust, if I think about it and start to analyze it, that why is it always the Jews who are picked on? We are not faultless, but who is? What other nation or faith or religion? I still believe that we did more for the world than any other race or religion did. If nothing else, we gave them their gods, even. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, you know, you cannot go into analyzing belief and religion, because if you do, you lose. You come across as a man who is a happy person. Would you say that's true?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm glad. Can we see some pictures of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. This man was my grandfather. The photo was taken in Mezonyarad in Hungary. The date of photo is early 1900s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Friedman, Miksa, and he died in 1944 in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the grandfather that you talked about on the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's the one. He was my mother's father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had 11 children. My mother was his oldest daughter. The lady on the left is my mother. Her name is [? Margit ?] Friedman, married Gruenfeld. The baby next to her is one of my older brothers. The one next to him is another brother of mine. The gentleman is my father. His name is Gruenfeld, Simon. The boy next to him is my oldest brother. His name is Erno. The one next to my father is Victor, and the baby is Tibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the [? 1914s ?] or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where? In Hajduhadhaz, Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: This little boy is my baby brother. His name is Gruenfeld, Imre. Picture was taken around 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gyongyos, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is me. This was taken in Gyongyos, Hungary, in about 1932.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what was your name then? My name was [? Endre ?] Gruenfeld.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my family. The young man on the left is my brother Tibor Gruenfeld. Next to him is my father, Simon Gruenfeld. Next to him is my mother, Margit Gruenfeld, and next to him is my sister-in-law, who became my sister-in-law after the war. Next to her is my brother Erno. And next to him, in the uniform, is my brother Victor. And this picture was taken in 1939 or '40 in Gyongyos, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not in it. I was taking the picture. Oh, and the little boy in front of them is my baby brother, Imre Gruenfeld

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you save this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was in Budapest at my aunt's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is me, [? Endre ?] Gruenfeld. This was taken by the Joint Distribution Committee in Budapest, Hungary, in 1945, probably in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, Hungary. This is the picture of our family. The first gentleman on the left is Ari [? Bachman, ?] my son-in-law. Next to him is Linda, his wife-- Linda [? Bachman ?] Goldenberg. Next to Linda is her brother Mark, Dr. Mark Goldenberg. Next to Mark is my wife, Yvette. Next to Yvette is Jeffrey Goldenberg. Next to Jeffrey is myself. Next to me is Larry Gardner. And next to Larry is Mark Gardner. This picture was taken in Detroit, Michigan, in 1979, on the occasion of Jeffrey's wedding.
